summary: This brief post traces the visual evolution of the OK button across twenty years of Windows operating systems, from Windows 3.0 through Windows Vista. While the button's appearance has changed significantly over time, the use of 'OK' has remained constant. Atwood explores the etymology of 'OK', citing research that traces it to 'oll korrect' - a product of a 1830s-1840s fad for comical abbreviations. The post connects everyday UI elements to deeper historical and linguistic roots.
key_points: The OK button has undergone significant visual changes across Windows versions while maintaining the same text
'OK' stands for 'oll korrect', originating from an 1830s-1840s fad for humorous misspelled abbreviations
Columbia professor Allen Walker Read definitively traced the etymology in academic articles from 1963-1964
The timing of OK's first written appearance in historical documents supports this origin theory
money_quotes: There is one constant, however: the use of OK.
The letters, not to keep you guessing, stand for 'oll korrect.'
They're the result of a fad for comical abbreviations that flourished in the late 1830s and 1840s.
key_insight: Even the most ubiquitous and seemingly simple UI elements like the OK button carry unexpected historical depth, connecting modern software to 19th-century linguistic fads.
